text: One thing I've learned from this series is that just like people, Hollywood meltdowns come in all different shapes and sizes. On the one hand, there's the spectacular implosions of Joss Weeden and Brie Larson, who reached the height of fame and success only to fall from grace in dramatic fashion. On the other hand, there's the Rachel Zigglers of the world, whose egos far outshine their talents and accomplishments, killing their careers before they even get off

the grounds. But there's another kind of crash and burn, dear viewer. a kind of slow-mo train wreck where you can see the pieces falling off even as it keeps plowing forwards until finally the whole thing derails and comes crashing to a halt. Which brings me neatly along to the story of Dwayne Douglas Johnson better known as The Rock. A man who once dominated the box office with the same intensity as he ruled the wrestling ring. Possibly the biggest and most

marketable celebrity on the planet who seemed to be everywhere doing everything and beloved by everyone. A man whose constantly upbeat, larger than-l life persona mast the hidden world of greed ego, lies, and hubris. A man so addicted to success and fame that it ended up consuming him. A man for whom the world itself was simply not enough. So join me for another episode of Crash and Burn as I tell you the Dwayne Johnson story. Dwayne Douglas Johnson was born in

California in 1972, the son of wrestling legend Rocky Johnson. And he wasn't the only wrestler in the family. His uncle his adopted grandfather, and even his grandmother were all in the business and it seemed like Dwayne was destined to follow them. He had different ideas though, and got into pro football instead. I mean, the guy was an absolute [ __ ] unit, 6'5 and 250 lbs at like 18 years old. So, who can blame him? He was a good athlete for sure, but he never

quite reached the big time in football and finally got cut loose after four years in the sport. With no high school qualifications and not many other options, he turned instead to wrestling used his family connections to fasttrack his way into the business and made his debut in the WWE the following year wrestling under the name Rocky Mvilla. And, on, paper,, at least, it, seemed, like, a great idea. His dad had been a fan favorite. He was young and athletic and

he was wrestling as a baby face, the kind of guy you're normally supposed to cheer for. Instead, it turned into a [ __ ] disaster. The fans could smell a manufactured star a mile away. They hated his generic good guy persona, and the harder the WWE tried to push him the more they rebelled against him. And when Dwayne was sidelined with a knee injury, it seemed like his wrestling career was over. Instead, he came back a few months later with a completely different personality, playing a cocky

arrogant heel, calling himself the Rock. He shit-talked everyone around him constantly referred to himself in the third person, and generally acted like he didn't give a [ __ ] anymore. It was an inspired decision and an instant hit tapping into the attitude era that was just kicking off at the time. And in the space of a few months, he went from one of the most hated wrestlers in the business to one of its most iconic superstars. He seemed to have a keen sense of what people wanted from him and

the ability to reshape his persona to suits it. Keep that in mind, dear viewer, because it'll be important later. Things were going great in the WWE. But Dwayne's aspirations extended way beyond the wrestling ring. Just like Hulk Hogan before him, he wanted to get into the movie business. And after playing the bad guy in 2001's The Mummy Returns, he landed his first lead role in the spin-off The Scorpion King. Dwayne turned out to be a natural on the big screen and the movie would go on to

make $180 million worldwide. Not bad for a firsttime actor. Unfortunately, this success wouldn't last. The next few years saw his career seesaw all over the place, doing everything from action to comedy, sports biopics to thrillers, and even Disney movies. None of it was really working, though. Most of his projects either flopped or barely broke even. Just like his early wrestling career, he couldn't quite find the persona that worked for him. Was he supposed to be a tough, nononsense hard

man, a family-friendly comedy actor, a commercial action star, a villain, a hero? Well, the answer finally came in 2011 when he joined the Fast and the Furious franchise, which itself was going through a bit of a redemption arc after struggling for years to figure out what it was supposed to be. His character, Luke Hobbs, was an instant hit with fans, tapping into the same charismatic anti-hero persona that had made The Rock so popular in wrestling a decade earlier. From then on, the Dwayne

Johnson hype train was finally running at full speed, and a wave of big budget highly successful action movies followed. For years, Hollywood producers had been looking for the next Arnold Schwarzenegger, the next definitive action star who would shape a whole generation of movies. And in Dwayne Johnson, it seemed they'd finally found what they were looking for. He was big and muscly. He was charismatic. He was marketable. He could navigate interviews and film junkets like a seasoned pro. He

was cool enough to appeal to adults, but cleancut and inoffensive enough to appeal to kids and families. He was somehow able to be everything to everyone and make it look easy. If Disney somehow made movie stars instead of movies, Dwayne Johnson is pretty much what they would churn out. And pretty soon, the [ __ ] guy was literally everywhere. Movies, commercials, brand deals, wrestling appearances. He was always there with a tailor made suit and a million dollar smile. Always excited

to promote his next big project. always ready with some inspiring words, always waiting to sell you something. But problems were a foot, dear viewer. The first signs of trouble came with rumors about his behavior on the Fast and the Furious movies. By all accounts, he was a dominant presence on set, and not in a good way. He wasted no time reshaping the movies to benefit himself, pushing for more lines and more screen time for his own character. He apparently had a

clause in his contract that says he couldn't lose a fight. and he even got into a public feud with Vin Diesel and accused him of being lazy, entitled, and a complete [ __ ] to work with. Naturally, this didn't gel too well with his smiling, easygoing public persona. And when he started taking heat for this post, he soon deleted it. Keep that one in mind, too, because the theme of Dwayne retreating from a position the moment he senses it could harm his image would become a bit of a theme in the

years aheads. The idea of him being an [ __ ] on set would really come up during the filming of Red One, where apparently he'd routinely show up late for filming with no explanation or apology. And I don't just mean 30 or 40 minutes late. I mean 7 or 8 hours. Jesus, that's basically an entire working day for normal people. These constant delays pushed back the schedule by weeks and added about $50 million to the film's budget. The weirdest thing about all this, though, was the piss

story. Rather than just going to the bathroom to take a leak like a normal person, Dwayne insisted on pissing into plastic bottles right there on set and handing them off to the crew to deal with. [Music] Like, imagine that for a second. Imagine it's your first time on a film set. You're all excited about being part of the magic of movie making and instead you find yourself hauling a leaky bottle filled with some asshole's urine. But that was only the beginning of Dwayne's

problems. People were beginning to notice things about all those big budget action movies he'd been churning out over the years. He was basically playing the same [ __ ] character in everything he did. Same personality, same general character arc, even the same clothes half the time. His movies were starting to be seen as the McDonald's Happy Meals of the film world, experienced by millions of people worldwide, but completely homogeneous and forgettable. What had started out as charming and

marketable was quickly becoming tiresome and predictable. The other thing people were realizing about Dwayne was that he lies a lot, often about the weirdest [ __ ] things, like how many calories he eats per day or claiming that he was visiting In-N-Out Burger for the first time three different times. Like, why would you even make something like that up in the first place? Who even gives a [ __ ] if you go to a burger restaurant? On the subject of stuff that Dwayne was

putting into his body, though, people were also starting to get a bit suspicious about his physique. Despite the fact that he was hurtling towards 50, he seemed to be getting bigger and more muscular with each passing year. And well, people were not buying it anymore. Allegations of steroid abuse were running wild. And the more he tried to play it down, the more they seemed to ramp up. Now, remember what I told you about Dwayne Taylor and his persona to match the situation? That kind of thing

definitely worked in the early days of his career when nobody was around to fact check him. But in the days of the internet and social media, well, people were beginning to realize that instead of having his own opinions about stuff he basically just said whatever he thought people wanted to hear. And if he got any kind of push back, he'd immediately fold like a house of cards and backtrack. The worst example was probably his Joe Rogan interview in 2023, where he basically spent three

hours giving evasive answers and reflecting the questions back at him. It was like Dwayne was the one doing the [ __ ] interviewing and it all fed into the public perception that nothing about the guy was real. He was more like a brand than a person. His every public appearance and sentiment carefully stage managed for maximum financial impacts. All was not well on the wrestling side of things either. See, ever since his jump to Hollywood, Dwayne had kept one

foot in the wrestling business as a kind of part-time superstar. anytime one of his movies flopped or he needed a bit of a career boost, he'd retreat back to the WWE for another run, make a ton of money, and then leave again. That strategy had been working pretty well until he showed up in 2024 and shoved his way into the middle of a long-running feud between Cody Rhodess and Roman Reigns. The fan backlash was so extreme that the WWE actually had to rewrite the whole storyline and put Cody

back in center stage where he belonged. In typical fashion, Dwayne would later claim that the whole thing was part of a grand plan to make Cody even more popular. I think I can smell shite. [ __ ] off Dwayne. Everyone could see him for exactly what he was now, an aging superstar trying to hold on to the spotlight beyond his time, even if it meant screwing over younger and more deserving people. The biggest disaster though, was 2022's Black Adam, which was supposed to be Dwayne's big entry into

the superhero genre. He'd made no secret of the fact that he was taking control of the DC EU and reshaping it with himself at the center. That he was going to do for DC what he'd done for the Fast and the Furious franchise and rehabilitate it. Yes, I can definitely smell shite. Well, that didn't work. The movie flopped big time and Black Adam was quickly buried along with the rest of the DCU. The Rock's big push into the superhero genre was over before it even

began. But that didn't stop Dwayne from desperately trying to cope about it claiming that the movie was actually a success if you looked at it the right way. Really? Yeah, sure, buddy. Losing hundreds of millions of dollars and nuking an entire franchise is a pretty loose definition of success. And so, we find ourselves in the year of our Lord 2025, where Dwayne Johnson has gone from the king of the Hollywood box office to a star in decline and a cautionary tale. He really

is a perfect example of having too much of a good thing that trying to please everyone ultimately ends up satisfying no one. And at the end of the day, all the fame and success in the world is no substitute for authenticity. I don't think he's a bad guy necessarily, and he's clearly done a lot of good things with his life and career. He just became so intoxicated by his larger than-l life persona and his desperate need to appeal to absolutely everyone that in the end

it kind of consumed him. So, what's next for The Rock? Is he going to bounce back, pivot, and find a new path to success or fade away into movie obscurity? Well, his upcoming film, The Smashing Machine, seems to signal a move away from generic big budget action slop and maybe a willingness to take on more challenging roles that play against type. So, who knows? The world does love a good redemption arc, and I don't think he's burned all of his bridges with

audiences yet. But, if there's one thing to take away from all this, perhaps it's time for him to stop being The Rock and finally start being Dwayne Johnson. If he can do that, perhaps there's still a way back for him. Anyway, that's all I've got for today. Go away now.
